introduction.purpose: The primary purpose of this document is to comprehensively define the functional and high-level user requirements for the **model manager** software tool. It delineates the core functionalities from the perspective of its end-users, primarily scientists and software engineers. This specification serves as a foundational input for the subsequent phases of system design, development, and validation, ensuring all stakeholders share a consistent understanding of the system's capabilities.
introduction.documentConventions: This document adheres to standard software requirements specification practices, particularly referencing the IEEE 830-1998 guidelines. Key terms and acronyms specific to the **model manager** domain, such as 'MM' for **model manager** and 'OTM' for '4DWX On the Move', are defined within the glossary to maintain clarity and consistency. Functional requirements are systematically enumerated with unique identifiers, facilitating traceability and reference throughout the software development lifecycle.
introduction.intendedAudience: The intended audience for this Software Requirements Specification includes a diverse group of stakeholders, ensuring all parties involved in the **model manager** project are well-informed. This encompasses prospective users, such as meteorologists and software engineers at NSAP and RAL, who will operate and interact with the system. Additionally, software development teams, project sponsors, and management will utilize this document to guide development, track progress, and make informed decisions regarding the system's implementation and strategic direction.
introduction.productScope: The **model manager** is a specialized software tool designed to streamline and automate the configuration, scheduling, execution, monitoring, and control of complex model jobs, including starting, stopping, and resuming operations. Its scope encompasses extending the existing model back-end system to enhance automation, particularly concerning node management across various computational clusters. The system primarily aims to simplify scientific modeling workflows, thereby improving efficiency and reducing manual intervention in critical environmental and climate modeling processes.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.
“4DWX on the Move (4DWX OTM)”, Scott Swerdlin, concept paper: http://sdg.rap.ucar.edu/wiki/index.php/Model_Manager#Documents

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **model manager** is conceived as a significant extension and enhancement to an existing model back-end system, addressing the growing need for automated and procedural approaches to setting up and running scientific model jobs. It functions both as a stand-alone application and as an integrated component of the larger **4DWX OTM** system. This dual operational mode allows for flexible deployment, catering to various organizational and project-specific needs while maintaining a unified approach to job management.
productFunctions: Set up and configure new model jobs, including **Weather FDDA** and **Climo** types.
Set up and configure 'post-processing' jobs for existing model output files.
Submit 'by-hand' jobs, providing flexibility for custom or pre-existing scripts and executables.
Load and submit job configurations from external files, enabling reproducibility and sharing.
Retrieve, modify, and resubmit previously saved job configurations for iterative workflows.
Monitor the status of scheduled, running, and historical jobs through a centralized interface.
Control active jobs, including options to stop, restart, or resume execution.
Manage node allocation and utilization across multiple computational clusters for optimized resource use.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: This group comprises experienced users deeply familiar with the model back-end systems and operational model runs. They require robust capabilities for setting up, maintaining, and customizing complex model jobs. Their expertise allows them to troubleshoot and optimize job configurations, often needing granular control over execution parameters and resource allocation. They are critical for ensuring the stability and performance of operational workflows.

These users are experienced with general model setups but may not be as involved in the daily operational maintenance. They primarily use the **model manager** for research purposes, often requiring the ability to set up and run customized model jobs with their own input data and pre-processors. They value flexibility in configuration and the ability to experiment with different model parameters without deeply understanding the underlying infrastructure.

This group consists of users who are less familiar with intricate model setups. Their primary needs revolve around monitoring the status of operational model runs, basic job control such as stopping and restarting existing jobs, and initiating 'standard' model jobs from predefined configurations. They require a highly intuitive and simplified interface, with minimal need for advanced customization or deep technical knowledge of the model back-end.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **model manager** is designed to operate within a distributed computing environment, interfacing with one or more computational clusters. It will be accessible via a **Web-based GUI** for general users and a **Command Line Tool** for power users and automation scripts. The backend infrastructure is expected to leverage standard operating systems prevalent in scientific computing, such as various distributions of **Linux**, for hosting computational workloads and managing cluster resources.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The system must integrate seamlessly with **MetVault** for input data retrieval and output data storage, while also supporting stand-alone operation without MetVault dependencies. The choice of programming languages and frameworks will prioritize compatibility with existing model back-end scripts and executables, primarily focusing on **Python**, **Bash**, and potentially **FORTRAN** or **C++** for core model executables. The architecture must accommodate a variety of model types, including **MM5**, **WRF**, **ClimoFDDA**, and **CAM**, necessitating a flexible and extensible job configuration schema. Furthermore, all components must operate efficiently within the resource constraints of high-performance computing clusters, including considerations for memory, CPU, and network bandwidth. The system must also support existing data formats and pre-processors, requiring adaptable interface layers.
userDocumentation: A comprehensive User Manual, providing step-by-step guides for all system features, including job setup, submission, monitoring, and control.
An Online Help System, offering contextual assistance for various UI elements and common tasks, accessible directly from the **Web-based GUI**.
Tutorials for common workflows, such as setting up a **Weather FDDA** job or a 'post-processing' job, aimed at different user expertise levels.
API Documentation for the **Command Line Tool**, detailing available commands, parameters, and expected outputs for advanced users and script automation.
A Troubleshooting Guide, addressing common issues, error messages, and their resolutions to minimize downtime and user frustration.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that underlying computational clusters and their job scheduling systems (e.g., **SLURM**, **PBS Pro**, **LSF**) are stable and accessible, providing the necessary compute resources for model execution. The availability and integrity of model back-end configuration files, input data, scripts, and executables on the designated clusters are also assumed. The system depends on the continued functionality and API stability of external systems such as **MetVault** for data archival and retrieval. Furthermore, it is assumed that users have appropriate network access and credentials to interact with the **Web-based GUI**, the **Command Line Tool**, and the target clusters. The successful execution of 'custom' jobs depends heavily on users providing correct and compatible scripts and executables that adhere to cluster environments.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **model manager** will offer two primary user interfaces: a **Web-based GUI** and a **Command Line Tool**. The **Web-based GUI** shall provide an intuitive, browser-agnostic interface for configuring, submitting, monitoring, and controlling model jobs, designed for both novice and experienced users. It shall adhere to modern web accessibility standards and maintain a consistent layout with clear navigation, standardized buttons, and informative error message displays. The **Command Line Tool** shall provide a robust, scriptable interface, enabling advanced users and automated systems to interact with the **model manager** functionalities directly. This tool will support complex command sequences, allowing for precise control over job parameters and integration into larger scripting workflows, catering to users who prefer programmatic interaction or require integration into CI/CD pipelines.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **model manager** will interface with computational clusters composed of various hardware platforms, including multi-core processors, high-speed memory, and potentially **GPU** accelerators for specific model types. The software shall manage the allocation and utilization of compute nodes and storage resources on these clusters, requiring interaction with underlying cluster resource managers. Data transfer to and from these hardware components will involve high-bandwidth network interfaces, ensuring efficient movement of large scientific datasets. The system will abstract hardware-specific details from the user, providing a unified job submission and monitoring experience across heterogeneous cluster environments.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **model manager** will integrate with several key software components to achieve its functionality. It shall interact with a **MetVault** system for storing and retrieving model input data, restart files, and output products via a defined API. Communication with the existing 'model back end system' will be essential for orchestrating job execution, requiring consistent interfaces for invoking configuration files, data input processors, and compiled model executables (e.g., **MM5**, **WRF**). The system will also interface with underlying cluster job schedulers (e.g., **SLURM**, **PBS Pro**) to submit, monitor, and manage computational tasks, necessitating a robust communication layer capable of handling job status updates and control signals. Custom user-provided scripts and executables must be supported, implying a flexible execution environment capable of securely running arbitrary user code within defined constraints.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **model manager** requires robust communication interfaces to support its distributed nature. The **Web-based GUI** will communicate with the **model manager** backend using standard **HTTP/HTTPS** protocols, ensuring secure data transfer and user authentication. The **Command Line Tool** will communicate via secure shell (**SSH**) or a dedicated **REST API** endpoint over **HTTPS** for programmatic access and automation. Internally, the **model manager** backend will communicate with various computational clusters using secure protocols, possibly involving **SSH** for remote command execution and data transfer, or specialized protocols for interaction with job schedulers. Message formatting for job status updates, notifications, and logging will adhere to defined standards (e.g., **JSON** or **XML**) to ensure interoperability. Email notification services will be integrated for sending job status alerts, utilizing standard **SMTP** protocols.

systemFeatures.name: Set Up and Submit a Model Job (Weather FDDA)
systemFeatures.description: This feature enables users, primarily meteorologists and software engineers, to configure and schedule **Weather FDDA** model jobs, specifically covering **GMOD** jobs, re-runs, and case studies. It automates the complex setup process, allowing users to define model parameters, input data sources, and execution environments with a high degree of customization or by using default configurations. The value lies in simplifying the creation of operational and research-oriented weather simulations, significantly reducing manual effort and potential for error in a critical scientific domain. This positively impacts the system by providing a flexible and robust framework for running diverse meteorological models, ensuring data integrity and computational efficiency.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User logs into the **model manager** system. Response: System presents available features, including 'Submit a new job'.
Stimulus: User selects 'Submit a new job' -> 'Set up a new model job' -> 'Weather FDDA'. Response: System displays options for configuring a **Weather FDDA** job.
Stimulus: User defines the cluster for job execution (optional). Response: System registers the cluster preference for the job.
Stimulus: User selects the model type (**MM5** or **WRF**). Response: System adapts configuration options based on the chosen model.
Stimulus: User defines a unique **JOBID**. Response: System validates and assigns the **JOBID** to the job.
Stimulus: User defines model domains (creates custom, chooses pre-defined, or submits **TERRAIN** files). Response: System processes domain configurations.
Stimulus: User specifies job run time and cycle. Response: System prompts for 'case study' or 're-run' if cycle time is past.
Stimulus: User provides job-specific information (e.g., cycle interval, forecast length). Response: System updates job parameters.
Stimulus: User specifies restart file writing frequency (optional). Response: System configures model to produce restart files.
Stimulus: User selects sigma-level configuration (predefined or custom). Response: System applies the chosen sigma-level settings.
Stimulus: User specifies the number of nodes (optional). Response: System attempts to allocate the specified nodes.
Stimulus: User opts for email notifications. Response: System configures alerts for job start, end, and termination.
Stimulus: User chooses IC/BC data sources (standard or custom). Response: System configures data retrieval from specified sources.
Stimulus: User provides custom IC/BC pre-processor or selects standard. Response: System applies the chosen pre-processing script.
Stimulus: User chooses additional pre-processors (e.g., **LDAS**) or skips. Response: System schedules specified additional pre-processing tasks.
Stimulus: User chooses obs data sources and processing (standard or custom). Response: System configures observational data handling.
Stimulus: User selects model executable options (for **MM5**) or defines model options (for **WRF**). Response: System configures the core model execution.
Stimulus: User chooses to run Final Analysis (optional). Response: System includes Final Analysis in the job workflow.
Stimulus: User chooses to run Prelim. Analysis (optional). Response: System includes Prelim. Analysis in the job workflow.
Stimulus: User chooses additional processing on model output (optional). Response: System schedules post-model output processing.
Stimulus: User opts to save model output in **MetVault** and specifies files. Response: System configures output archival in **MetVault**.
Stimulus: User chooses to save and submit the job now. Response: System saves the configuration and immediately initiates pre-processing and model run.
Stimulus: User chooses to run post-processing. Response: System transitions to post-processing configuration (refer to MM-UC-002).
Stimulus: User saves the job configuration to a file. Response: System stores the configuration persistently.
Stimulus: User submits the job. Response: System schedules the job for execution.
functionalRequirements: The system shall present options for 'Weather FDDA' and 'Climo' when setting up a new model job.
The system shall allow the user to optionally specify a target cluster for the job.
The system shall allow the user to select between **MM5** or **WRF** as the model to be used.
The system shall enforce a unique alphanumeric **JOBID** for each submitted job.
The system shall provide options for creating custom domains, choosing from pre-defined domains, or submitting custom **TERRAIN** files.
The system shall prompt for 'case study' or 're-run' designation if a past cycle time is selected.
The system shall allow specification of cycle interval, forecast length, and other job-specific parameters.
The system shall enable configuration of restart file writing frequency.
The system shall offer a choice between predefined sigma-level configurations and user-supplied custom configurations.
The system shall allow optional specification of the number of compute nodes for the job.
The system shall provide an option to receive email notifications upon job start, completion, and termination.
The system shall support both standard (e.g., **ETA**, **AVNFTP**, **GFS004**) and custom (e.g., host:Full_Path_to_Dir) IC/BC data sources.
The system shall retrieve input data for re-runs from **MetVault** based on the original cycle's availability.
The system shall allow selection between standard and custom IC/BC pre-processing scripts.
The system shall offer additional pre-processor options like **LDAS** or allow user-supplied custom pre-processors.
The system shall support standard (e.g., **WMO**, **SAMS**) and custom observational data sources and processors.
The system shall retrieve observational data for re-runs from **MetVault** based on the original cycle's availability.
The system shall present model-specific options, including choice of pre-compiled **MM5** executables based on domain size/nodes, or **WRF** model options (TBD).
The system shall allow the user to optionally run Final Analysis for re-runs and case studies.
The system shall allow the user to optionally run Prelim. Analysis for re-runs and case studies.
The system shall allow the user to optionally configure additional processing on model output (TBD: Bias Correction options).
The system shall provide an option to save model output in **MetVault**, requiring specification of which output files to store.
The system shall allow immediate saving and submission of the job configuration, initiating IC/BC, obs processing, and model run.
The system shall allow the user to save the complete job configuration to a file.
The system shall submit the configured job for execution.

systemFeatures.name: Set Up and Submit a Model Job (ClimoFDDA)
systemFeatures.description: This feature integrates **GCAT** functionalities within the **model manager**, allowing meteorologists and software engineers to set up and run **ClimoFDDA** jobs. It streamlines the creation of climatological simulations by providing options for defining domains, timelines, and ensemble parameters. The value lies in centralizing climatological modeling workflows, enhancing reproducibility, and facilitating complex ensemble analyses directly within the **model manager** environment. This contributes to the system's extensibility by incorporating specialized modeling capabilities and expanding its utility for diverse scientific applications.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects 'Submit a new job' -> 'Set up a new model job' -> 'Climo'. Response: System displays options for configuring a **ClimoFDDA** job.
Stimulus: User defines a unique **JOBID**. Response: System validates and assigns the **JOBID**.
Stimulus: User supplies job parameters as per **GCAT** tool. Response: System applies the specified parameters.
Stimulus: User determines the domain location. Response: System configures the geographical domain for the job.
Stimulus: User specifies locations for pseudo-observations and custom cross sections. Response: System registers these locations for potential post-processing.
Stimulus: User picks a pre-configured **MM5** setup. Response: System loads the selected **MM5** configuration.
Stimulus: User sets the job timeline (start time, end time, years). Response: System configures the temporal scope of the simulation.
Stimulus: User sets ensemble options (e.g., hourly, min/max/mean, diurnal cycle). Response: System applies the specified ensemble configurations.
Stimulus: User requests a specific number of nodes (optional). Response: System attempts to allocate the specified nodes.
Stimulus: User chooses to run additional processing on model output. Response: System configures the additional output processing (TBD).
Stimulus: User opts to save model output in **MetVault** and specifies which output. Response: System configures archival of specified member/ensemble output in **MetVault**.
Stimulus: User chooses to save and submit the job now. Response: System saves configuration and initiates member models, ensemble processing, and optional output processing.

Stimulus: User chooses to run post-processing on ensemble output and selects options (Plots, NAPS, MDV, Sites, MEDOC, Raster, PRF, Wind Roses). Response: System presents further options for each selected output product.
Stimulus: User supplies destination host and location for each output product. Response: System configures file transfer for post-processed outputs.
Stimulus: User specifies post-processing for member outputs (year, post-processor(s), destination). Response: System schedules member output post-processing tasks.
Stimulus: User specifies running another process (coupled app) on post-processed output (TBD). Response: System integrates the coupled application execution into the workflow.
Stimulus: User saves the job configuration to a file. Response: System stores the configuration persistently.
Stimulus: User submits the job. Response: System schedules the job for execution.
functionalRequirements: The system shall present 'Weather FDDA' and 'Climo' options when setting up a new model job.
The system shall allow the user to define a unique alphanumeric **JOBID** for the **ClimoFDDA** job.
The system shall accept and apply job parameters as specified by the **GCAT** tool (TBD: Define specific **GCAT** parameters).
The system shall enable the user to determine the geographical domain location for the job.
The system shall allow specification of locations for pseudo-observations and custom cross sections.
The system shall provide a selection of pre-configured **MM5** setups.
The system shall allow the user to set the job timeline, including start time, end time, and specific years.
The system shall offer ensemble options, including frequency (e.g., hourly, daily (TBD)), statistical computations (min, max, mean, standard deviation), diurnal cycle, and typical moment.
The system shall allow the user to request a specific number of compute nodes for the job.
The system shall provide an option to run additional processing on the model output (TBD: Applicability to **ClimoFDDA**).
The system shall allow the user to save model output in **MetVault**, specifying which output (member and/or ensemble) to send.
The system shall allow immediate saving and submission of the job, initiating member models, ensemble processing, and optional model output processing.
The system shall present post-processing options for ensemble output, including Plots (**NCL** or **RIP**), **NAPS**, **MDV**, Sites, **MEDOC** (1–4), Raster, **PRF**, and Wind Roses.
The system shall prompt for a destination host and location for each selected post-processed output product.
The system shall allow specification of post-processing for member outputs, including year-output, post-processor(s) to use, and destination for output files.
The system shall allow the user to specify whether another coupled application should run on post-processed output products (TBD: Further clarification needed).
The system shall allow the user to save the complete job configuration to a file.
The system shall submit the configured job for execution.

systemFeatures.name: Set Up and Submit a Post-Processing Job
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows users, particularly meteorologists and software engineers, to initiate post-processing tasks on existing model output files. It provides a dedicated workflow for applying various data conversions, calculations, and visualizations to previously generated model data, distinct from a full model run. The value lies in enabling flexible analysis and visualization of scientific results, decoupling post-processing from initial model execution and supporting iterative data exploration. This improves system efficiency by allowing specialized tasks to be performed on demand, without re-running entire simulations, and provides enhanced data utility.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects 'Submit a new job' -> 'Set up a new post-processing job'. Response: System prompts for location and name of the model output file.
Stimulus: User supplies **JOBID** and cycle time for a running/scheduled **FDDA** job. Response: System identifies the target model output file.
Stimulus: User supplies the location of an existing model output file. Response: System validates access to the specified file.
Stimulus: User chooses the type of post-processing (e.g., Plots, NAPS, MDV). Response: System prepares the interface for selected post-processing options.
Stimulus: User supplies a custom configuration file or uses default for each option. Response: System applies the chosen configuration for post-processing.
Stimulus: User supplies the destination location for output products. Response: System configures the output path for generated files.
Stimulus: User specifies the number of nodes (optional). Response: System attempts to allocate the specified nodes for post-processing (TBD).
Stimulus: User saves the job's settings to a file. Response: System persistently stores the post-processing job configuration.
Stimulus: User submits the job. Response: System schedules the post-processing task for execution.
functionalRequirements: The system shall present 'Set up a new post-processing job' as an option for submitting a new job.
The system shall prompt the user to supply the location and name of the model output file for post-processing.
The system shall allow identification of model output files by **JOBID** and cycle time for running or scheduled **FDDA** jobs.
The system shall allow specification of the location for already existing model output files.
The system shall offer various types of post-processing, including Plots (**NCL** or **RIP**), **NAPS**, **MDV**, Sites, **MEDOC** (1–4), Stereo, and Verification.
The system shall allow the user to supply a custom configuration file or use a default for each post-processing option.
The system shall require the user to specify a destination location for the post-processed output products.
The system shall allow optional specification of the number of nodes for the post-processing job (TBD: Confirm requirement).
The system shall allow the user to save the post-processing job's settings to a file.
The system shall submit the configured post-processing job for execution.

systemFeatures.name: Submit a 'By-hand' Job
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides flexibility for meteorologists and software engineers to register and run customized jobs that were set up independently on a cluster, without using the **model manager**'s Job-Setup module. It accommodates existing workflows and allows for highly specialized, user-defined operations, extending the system's utility beyond its predefined model types. The value lies in its ability to integrate external, custom computational tasks into the **model manager**'s monitoring and management framework, offering centralized oversight for diverse job types. This enhances the system's adaptability by supporting legacy and experimental workflows.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects 'Submit a new job' -> 'Submit a 'By Hand' Job'. Response: System prompts for mandatory and optional job information.

Stimulus: User supplies mandatory information: **JOBID**, script location, run time, estimated run time, executable names, max runtime, number of nodes, output product location. Response: System validates and registers the mandatory job details.
Stimulus: User supplies optional information: frequency, job type, additional info. Response: System records the optional job details.
Stimulus: User saves the job's settings to a file. Response: System persistently stores the 'by-hand' job configuration.
Stimulus: User submits the job. Response: System registers the job and schedules it for execution.
Stimulus: User views the job queue. Response: System displays the 'by-hand' job along with other jobs.
Stimulus: User opts for email notifications. Response: System configures alerts for job start, finish, or termination.
functionalRequirements: The system shall present 'Submit a 'By Hand' Job' as an option for submitting a new job.
The system shall require the user to provide a mandatory **JOBID** for the 'by-hand' job.
The system shall require the user to provide the mandatory location of the script (host:/full_path_to_script).
The system shall require the user to provide the mandatory time when the script is to run.
The system shall require the user to provide the mandatory estimated time for the script run.
The system shall require the user to provide the mandatory name(s) of executable(s).
The system shall require the user to provide the mandatory maximum runtime for the executable(s).
The system shall require the user to provide the mandatory number of nodes for the job (TBD: Confirm requirement).
The system shall require the user to provide the mandatory location of output products (host:/dir_path).
The system shall allow optional specification of job run frequency.
The system shall allow optional specification of job type.
The system shall allow optional inclusion of any additional information.
The system shall allow the user to save the 'by-hand' job's settings to a file.
The system shall submit the configured 'by-hand' job for execution.
The system shall display the submitted 'by-hand' job in the job queue.
The system shall send email notifications to the user upon job start, finish, or termination.

systemFeatures.name: Load and Submit Job Configuration from File
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides meteorologists and software engineers with the capability to load a job configuration from a specified file directly into the **model manager**. It streamlines the process of submitting pre-defined or shared job configurations, ensuring consistency and reproducibility across different users or environments. The value lies in facilitating collaboration and standardizing job setups, reducing the need for manual re-entry of complex parameters. This enhances system usability by providing an efficient mechanism for deploying known configurations.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User selects 'Submit a new job' -> 'Submit a job configuration file'. Response: System prompts for the file name to load.
Stimulus: User supplies the file name. Response: System loads the job configuration from the specified file.
Stimulus: User makes changes to the loaded configuration. Response: System updates the job parameters in memory.
Stimulus: User saves the changed configuration. Response: System persistently stores the modified configuration.
Stimulus: User submits the job. Response: System schedules the job for execution based on the loaded (and potentially modified) configuration.
functionalRequirements: The system shall present 'Submit a job configuration file' as an option for submitting a new job.
The system shall prompt the user to supply the file name containing the job configuration.
The system shall load the job configuration from the specified file.
The system shall allow the user to make changes to the loaded configuration.
The system shall allow the user to save the changed configuration.
The system shall submit the job based on the loaded or modified configuration.

systemFeatures.name: Retrieve and Run Previously Saved Job Configuration
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows meteorologists and software engineers to access, modify, and re-run job configurations that were previously saved within the **model manager**. It promotes efficient iteration on scientific experiments and operational workflows by eliminating the need to re-enter parameters for recurring or similar tasks. The value lies in enhancing productivity and ensuring the reproducibility of past analyses, making it easier for users to manage their personal or team-wide job configurations. This improves system efficiency by leveraging historical data and configurations.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User logs in with user ID and password. Response: System authenticates the user.
Stimulus: User chooses to view previously saved job configurations. Response: System displays a table of saved jobs with attributes like Job ID, Type, Cycle Time, and Last Run Time.
Stimulus: User selects a job. Response: System loads the configuration of the selected job.
Stimulus: User changes the job configuration. Response: System updates the job parameters in memory.
Stimulus: User saves the changed job configuration. Response: System persistently stores the modified configuration.
Stimulus: User submits the job. Response: System schedules the job for execution based on the (potentially modified) configuration.
functionalRequirements: The system shall require user authentication (user ID/password) to access saved job configurations.
The system shall display a list of previously saved job configurations, including attributes such as Job ID, Job type, last run cycle time (if applicable), and last run time.
The system shall allow the user to select a saved job configuration.
The system shall provide options to change or delete the selected job configuration.
The system shall allow the user to modify the parameters of a retrieved job configuration.
The system shall allow the user to save the modified job configuration.
The system shall submit the retrieved and potentially modified job configuration for execution.

systemFeatures.name: View Scheduled, Running, and Old Jobs
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides meteorologists and software engineers with a centralized interface to monitor the status of all jobs managed by the **model manager**, including those scheduled, actively running, or completed in the past. It offers critical transparency into system operations and job progress, enabling users to track their simulations and manage resources effectively. The value lies in providing comprehensive visibility and control over computational tasks, facilitating rapid identification of issues, and informed decision-making regarding resource allocation. This enhances system observability and operational control for all stakeholders.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User logs in with user ID and password. Response: System authenticates the user.
Stimulus: User chooses to view running, scheduled, past, or all jobs. Response: System filters and displays a job table based on the user's selection.
Stimulus: User selects a job from the table. Response: System displays more detailed information about the selected job (TBD).
Stimulus: User chooses to view a job's log files. Response: System displays the log output for the selected job.
Stimulus: User deletes their job from the job queue. Response: System removes the job from the queue (restricted to owner or super user).
Stimulus: User (or super user) stops a running job. Response: System terminates the execution of the selected running job.
Stimulus: User (or super user) restarts a job. Response: System re-initiates the selected job from its beginning.
Stimulus: User (or super user) resumes a stopped job. Response: System continues the execution of the selected stopped job from its last known state.
functionalRequirements: The system shall require user authentication (user ID/password) to access job monitoring features.
The system shall allow users to filter job views by status: running, scheduled, past, or all jobs.

The system shall present a job table displaying attributes such as user ID, Job ID, Job type, Job priority (TBD), start time, estimated remaining time/total run time, cycle (if applicable), stage (e.g., Pre-processing), status (SCHEDULED, RUNNING, %, DONE), cluster and nodes (for running jobs), and number of processors.
The system shall provide an option to view detailed information for a selected job (TBD: Define specific details).
The system shall allow users to view the log files associated with any of their jobs.
The system shall allow a user to delete their own jobs from the job queue.
The system shall allow a 'super user' to delete any job from the job queue.
The system shall allow a user to stop their own running job.
The system shall allow a 'super user' to stop any running job.
The system shall allow a user to restart their own job.
The system shall allow a 'super user' to restart any job.
The system shall allow a user to resume their own stopped job.
The system shall allow a 'super user' to resume any stopped job.

performanceRequirements: The **model manager** Web-based GUI shall respond to user interactions (e.g., form submissions, table filtering) within 2 seconds under typical load (up to 50 concurrent users). This is crucial for user experience and efficient workflow. Model job submission, including validation and initial scheduling, shall complete within 5 seconds for standard configurations. Complex job configurations with extensive parameter sets should not exceed 10 seconds. The system shall initiate model jobs on target clusters within 30 seconds of submission under normal cluster load, ensuring timely execution of scientific tasks. Monitoring data (job status, logs) shall be updated and displayed in the GUI with a latency of no more than 10 seconds from the cluster's actual state. This ensures users have near real-time visibility into their computational tasks.
safetyRequirements: The system shall implement robust input validation mechanisms for all job configuration parameters to prevent the execution of malformed or unsafe commands on computational clusters. This is essential to prevent system instability or unintended resource consumption. Critical job control actions (stop, restart, resume, delete) shall require explicit user confirmation and be subject to role-based access controls to prevent accidental or unauthorized manipulation of active simulations. The **model manager** shall ensure that erroneous job configurations or execution failures do not lead to data corruption or loss of existing model output on shared storage systems. It must gracefully handle external cluster outages, protecting its internal state and queue, and providing clear error notifications to affected users.
securityRequirements: The **model manager** shall implement strong user authentication through a username/password mechanism, potentially integrating with existing organizational identity management systems. All communications between the client interfaces (**Web-based GUI**, **Command Line Tool**) and the **model manager** backend shall be encrypted using **TLS 1.2** or higher to protect sensitive job configurations and user credentials. Role-based access control (RBAC) shall be enforced, differentiating between standard users (owning their jobs) and 'super users' (managing all jobs) for critical operations like stopping or deleting jobs. Access to underlying cluster resources shall be mediated and authorized by the **model manager**, preventing direct unauthorized user access to compute nodes. User-provided scripts for 'by-hand' jobs shall be executed within a secure, sandboxed environment on the clusters to mitigate the risk of malicious code injection or privilege escalation.
softwareQualityAttributes: **Reliability**: The **model manager** shall demonstrate high operational reliability, ensuring that submitted jobs are correctly scheduled and executed without unexpected interruptions or data discrepancies. The system shall recover gracefully from transient network or cluster failures, resuming operations or alerting users as appropriate. This is vital for maintaining the integrity of scientific research and operational forecasting. **Maintainability**: The system's codebase shall be modular and well-documented, adhering to established coding standards to facilitate future enhancements, bug fixes, and integration with new model versions or cluster technologies. This ensures long-term viability and reduces total cost of ownership. **Scalability**: The **model manager** shall be designed to scale horizontally, capable of managing an increasing number of concurrent users and jobs across a growing number of computational clusters without significant degradation in performance. This is critical for accommodating expanding research and operational demands. **Usability**: The **Web-based GUI** shall be intuitive and user-friendly, minimizing the learning curve for new users while providing efficient workflows for experienced operators. The **Command Line Tool** shall offer clear syntax and informative feedback, supporting automation. This enhances user productivity and reduces training costs. **Robustness**: The system shall be resilient to invalid inputs, unexpected environmental conditions, and external system failures, preventing crashes or data corruption. It shall provide clear error messages and recovery options to users. This ensures stability and trustworthiness in scientific operations.
businessRules: All jobs submitted to the **model manager** must have a unique alphanumeric **JOBID** for identification and tracking purposes across its lifecycle. Job prioritization (TBD: Define specific prioritization rules and mechanisms) shall be implemented to ensure critical operational model runs are given precedence over research or lower-priority tasks. The **model manager** shall enforce resource allocation policies (e.g., maximum nodes per user, job duration limits) on computational clusters to prevent monopolization of resources. Output data retention policies (e.g., how long 'old jobs' data and logs are stored) shall be defined to manage storage capacity effectively. User authentication and authorization for job control actions must adhere to organizational security policies and privilege levels.

glossary.definition: Acronym for **model manager**, the software tool defined in this document.
Acronym for '4DWX On the Move', a larger system that the **model manager** may be a part of.
A collective term describing the configuration files, data input, scripts, and executables necessary to run model jobs on a cluster.
A collection of processes that compute a weather or climate model over a given domain and for a specific time period.
A type of model job, specifically either a 'ClimoFDDA'-job (**GCAT**) or a **CAM** job.
The current operational **RTFDDA** job utilizing either **MM5** or **WRF** models.
Refers to a **GMOD** job or an **RTFDDA** ensemble run.
A re-run of a previous **RTFDDA** cycle or a dedicated case study.
A set of processes that take model output files as input, perform calculations or data conversions, and potentially produce new output files in a different format.
An existing set of scripts and executables, pre-configured by the user on a cluster, which can run any executable and is registered with the **model manager**.
A unique identifier assigned to each model or processing job submitted to the **model manager**.
An external data storage and retrieval system that the **model manager** interacts with for model inputs and outputs.
Initial Conditions and Boundary Conditions data, critical inputs for running meteorological models.
A commonly used regional atmospheric model (Fifth-Generation Penn State/NCAR Mesoscale Model).
A widely used atmospheric research and forecasting model (Weather Research and Forecasting Model).
A tool or framework for setting up and running **ClimoFDDA** jobs, whose functionalities are integrated into the **model manager**.
A specific type of climate model (Community Atmosphere Model) that the **model manager** is intended to support.
Acronym for a project or organization related to the context of model manager operations.
Acronym for a research laboratory or organization related to the context of model manager operations.
To Be Determined; indicates an item whose details are not yet finalized and require further definition.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart illustrates the high-level process of job submission within the **model manager**. Nodes represent distinct steps or decisions in the workflow, with their shapes indicating their nature (e.g., rounded rectangles for start/end, rectangles for processes, diamonds for decisions). Arrows denote the flow of control. All node labels with spaces are enclosed in double quotes for strict Mermaid compliance. Node IDs are alphanumeric, without spaces or reserved words like 'end'. The direction is specified as Top-Down (TD).
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram illustrates the detailed interaction for setting up and submitting a 'Weather FDDA' model job. Participants represent active entities in the system, and messages show the flow of communication over time. Activations (represented by `+`/`-` or explicit `activate`/`deactivate` pairs if necessary) indicate active processing periods for participants. Messages are simple and direct, avoiding special characters. Participant aliases are unquoted. The diagram emphasizes the step-by-step user interaction with the **model manager** and its subsequent interaction with internal components.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant User
  participant GUI as "Web-based GUI"
  participant MM as "Model Manager Backend"
  participant ClusterMgr as "Cluster Manager"
  participant MetVault as "MetVault"

  User->>GUI: Login Credentials
  GUI->>MM: Authenticate(User)
  MM->>GUI: Authentication Result

  alt Successful Login
    User->>GUI: "Submit a new job" -> "Set up a new model job" -> "Weather FDDA"
    GUI->>MM: RequestWeatherFDDASetup()
    MM->>GUI: DisplayWeatherFDDAParams()

    User->>GUI: Input Parameters (JOBID, Model, Domains, etc.)
    GUI->>MM: ValidateAndConfigureJob(JobConfig)
    MM->>GUI: ConfigurationStatus

    User->>GUI: Specify IC/BC Data Source
    GUI->>MM: ConfigureInputData(Source, Preprocessor)
    MM->>MetVault: QueryData(Source, Cycle)
    MetVault-->>MM: ReturnDataAvailability
    MM->>GUI: DataConfigStatus

    User->>GUI: Select Save & Submit Option
    GUI->>MM: SaveAndSubmitJob(JobConfig)
    MM->>MM: PersistJobConfiguration(JobConfig)
    MM->>ClusterMgr: RequestJobExecution(JobConfig)
    ClusterMgr->>MM: JobSubmissionReceipt
    MM->>GUI: JobSubmittedConfirmation
    GUI->>User: "Job MM-REQ-001 submitted."
  else Unsuccessful Login
    GUI->>User: "Invalid Credentials"
  end

  MM->>ClusterMgr: MonitorJobStatus(JOBID)
  ClusterMgr-->>MM: UpdateJobStatus(JOBID, Status, Progress)
  MM->>GUI: NotifyJobStatusChange(JOBID, Status)
  GUI->>User: "Job MM-REQ-001 is RUNNING (50%)"
sequenceDiagram.caption: Weather FDDA Job Submission Sequence

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This ER diagram models the key entities within the **model manager** domain and their relationships. Entities are represented by rectangles with their attributes listed below. Each attribute includes its data type and key designation (PK for Primary Key, FK for Foreign Key, UK for Unique Key). Relationship types and cardinalities are explicitly defined using standard ERD notation (e.g., `||--o{` for one-to-many, `}|--||` for many-to-one). All relationship labels are strictly quoted. Entity names with spaces are quoted; attribute names are alphanumeric and unquoted. Foreign keys imply a many-to-one relationship from the entity containing the FK to the entity containing the PK.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
    USER ||--o{ "JOB CONFIGURATION" : "manages"
    USER ||--o{ JOB : "owns"
    "JOB CONFIGURATION" ||--o{ JOB : "defines"
    CLUSTER }|--|| JOB : "runs on"
    JOB ||--o{ "LOG ENTRY" : "generates"
    "JOB CONFIGURATION" }|--|| "MODEL TYPE" : "specifies"
    "MODEL TYPE" { 
        int id PK
        string name UK
    }
    USER {
        int user_id PK
        string username UK
        string email
        string password_hash
    }
    JOB {
        int job_id PK
        int user_id FK
        int config_id FK
        int cluster_id FK
        int model_type_id FK
        string job_name
        string status
        datetime start_time
        datetime end_time
        decimal estimated_runtime
        string output_location
        string job_parameters
    }
    "JOB CONFIGURATION" {
        int config_id PK
        int user_id FK
        int model_type_id FK
        string config_name UK
        text config_data
        datetime created_at
    }
    CLUSTER {
        int cluster_id PK
        string name UK
        string location
        string ip_address
        int total_nodes
        int available_nodes
    }
    "LOG ENTRY" {
        int log_id PK
        int job_id FK
        datetime timestamp
        string level
        text message
    }
