The system shall process vehicle mode changes during minor frame 49 (NX-REQ-021).
The system shall execute cyclic tasks, providing an **API** call to allow the application to block until its next iteration (NX-REQ-022).
The system shall execute as the highest priority FTSS or application task in the system (NX-REQ-024).
The system shall maintain a minor frame count from 0 to 49 (NX-REQ-025).
The system shall assign tasks priority values according to their rate, with higher rates receiving higher priority (NX-REQ-027).
The system shall detect 50 Hz, 10 Hz, and 1 Hz rate group over-runs (NX-REQ-028).
The system shall report rate group over-runs to the application via an **API** service for incorporation in the telemetry data stream (NX-REQ-029).
The system shall provide an **API** call to specify which task was running within the rate group that over-ran (NX-REQ-216).
The system shall provide a mechanism to inform a task when it did not complete during the previous frame and restart it at the beginning of the task (NX-REQ-030).

The system shall set the 50 Hz interval timer to a count down value to cause the next minor frame interrupt at 20 msec from the previous interrupt congruently in all operational **FCPs** (NX-REQ-181).
The system shall issue a 50 Hz interrupt to the **ICPs** by means of a **VMEbus IRQ5** interrupt (NX-REQ-032).
The system shall issue the 50 Hz interrupt to all the **ICPs** with a skew no greater than 330 microseconds (NX-REQ-191).
The system shall send the minor frame number, vehicle mode, mission elapsed time (MET), and separation elapsed time (SEP) to the **ICP** prior to the 50 Hz interrupt (NX-REQ-033).
The system shall execute scheduler and Time Services FTSS overhead tasks in each rate group in no longer than 1 millisecond (NX-REQ-034).
The system shall provide an **API** call that provides the application program the minor frame number (NX-REQ-278).

The system shall provide rate groups that execute at 50 Hz, 10 Hz, and 1 Hz, with a drift rate no greater than 50 microseconds per second, and with a jitter no greater than 330 microseconds (NX-REQ-035).
The system shall provide a method to schedule tasks at a set rate and in a set order within the rate group (NX-REQ-037).
The system shall execute all the tasks in each of the rate groups that have been installed in the current mode (NX-REQ-198).
The system shall rely on the order used in adding tasks to a rate group to determine task priorities (NX-REQ-039).

The system shall provide a method for a task to be scheduled as a 50 Hz "helper" task for source congruency input exchanges and voted output exchanges that starts in a particular minor frame but runs only during every 5th or 50th minor frame (NX-REQ-042).
The system shall provide a task deadline capability that allows the application to specify which minor frame a task should start in and finish in (NX-REQ-270).
The behavior of synchronous tasks executed by the scheduler shall be deterministic (NX-REQ-043).

systemFeatures.name: Memory Management and Exception Handling
systemFeatures.description: This feature ensures the integrity and protection of the **nasa x38** system's memory, isolating software faults and managing hardware-induced memory violations. It implements a policy of task restart upon detection of memory exceptions, preventing propagation of errors and maintaining system stability. By providing a robust exception handling mechanism, the FTSS ensures that both software and hardware anomalies are detected, reported, and contained, minimizing their impact on mission-critical operations. This proactive approach to memory management is fundamental to the fault-tolerant design.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A software or hardware exception occurs during task execution. Response: The system detects the exception, makes the error type and context data available to the application via an **API**, and either restarts the offending task or returns from the handler.
Stimulus: A memory violation (exception) is detected. Response: The system restarts the task that was executing when the violation occurred, as per the defined policy.
Stimulus: A software exception occurs within the FTSS itself. Response: The system makes error type and context data available to the application, then restarts the offending task from its beginning.
Stimulus: A software exception occurs during system startup. Response: The FTSS issues a **VME** reset to the **FCR** where the exception occurred, initiating a controlled restart of that component.
functionalRequirements: The system shall, upon occurrence of any exception, make the error type and all relevant context data (Machine State Register, Save/Restore Registers SRR0 & SRR1) available to the application via an **API** service for telemetry (NX-REQ-172).
The system shall provide a mechanism for a task to optionally define a user-written software-exception-handling routine that runs in the context of the task (NX-REQ-031).

The system shall, for hardware exceptions and reserved exceptions, make the error type and context data available to the application, then return from the exception handler to the task that was running when the exception occurred (NX-REQ-276).
The system shall, for software exceptions occurring within the FTSS, make the error type and context data available to the application, then restart the offending task at its beginning (NX-REQ-277).

The system shall, for other software exceptions, after making available the error type and context data to the application, resume processing at the initialization point of the offending task (NX-REQ-173).
The system shall, for software exceptions occurring during Startup, issue a **VME** reset to the **FCR** in which the exception occurred (NX-REQ-301).
The system shall, upon detection of a memory violation (exception), restart the task that is currently executing (NX-REQ-044).

systemFeatures.name: Communication Services
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides robust and fault-tolerant message-passing capabilities, enabling seamless data exchange between tasks within and across virtual groups. It abstracts the underlying packet-based network element hardware, offering synchronous and immediate communication services through message queue and pipe sockets. This critical functionality ensures congruent data delivery among redundant processors and provides mechanisms for broadcasting messages and detecting network anomalies, underpinning the system's ability to maintain a consistent state across its distributed components, even in the presence of faults.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: An application task initiates a synchronous message transfer via a message queue socket. Response: The system queues the outgoing message and transmits it at rate group frame boundaries, ensuring consistent message delivery.

Stimulus: An application task initiates an immediate message transfer via a pipe socket. Response: The system immediately transfers the message, potentially bypassing the network element for intra-virtual group communication or forcing immediate **NE** access for inter-virtual group communication.

Stimulus: A babbling **NE** or **ICP** transmits erroneous packets. Response: The system detects the erroneous packets within 20 milliseconds and masks out the babbling component within 40 milliseconds to prevent data corruption.
Stimulus: A task attempts to send a message but the message queue is full. Response: The system returns an error to the corresponding task, indicating insufficient buffer space.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide "synchronous" message passing services in the form of "message queues" (NX-REQ-047).
The system shall provide "immediate" message passing services in the form of "pipes" for fast data throughput with minimal latency (NX-REQ-048).
The system shall provide the capability to "broadcast" messages to all virtual groups (NX-REQ-049).
The system shall restrict the use of "immediate" message passing services between virtual groups (from **FCP** to **ICP**) to tasks running in the highest rate group on the **FCP** (NX-REQ-050).
The system shall detect message passing between application tasks living on the same virtual group and bypass the usage of the network element (NX-REQ-051).
The system shall route messages to the proper virtual group(s) and socket (NX-REQ-052).
The system shall deliver messages in the same order at each member of a virtual group (NX-REQ-053).
The system shall perform synchronous message passing at rate group frame boundaries (NX-REQ-054).
The system shall detect a babbling **NE** or **ICP** within 20 milliseconds of the receipt of the first erroneous packet (NX-REQ-235).
The system shall mask out a babbling **NE** or **ICP** within 40 milliseconds after it is detected (NX-REQ-255).
The system shall, for synchronous message passing sockets, queue outgoing messages until they are transmitted at frame boundaries (NX-REQ-055).
The system shall, if there is insufficient space to enqueue a message for transmission, return an error to the corresponding task (NX-REQ-059).
The system shall provide a message queue communication mechanism that guarantees message delivery between a sending and receiving task (NX-REQ-062).
The system shall provide an **API** for "message queue" communication (NX-REQ-063).

The system shall provide error handling information as feedback to "message queue" **API** calls, including notification of invalid parameters, broadcast attempt errors, "open" endpoint errors, full queue, connection/transmission errors, creation/open failures, and truncated messages (NX-REQ-064).
The system shall only allow a single task living on each specified virtual group to "open" the respective end of a message queue (NX-REQ-066).
The system shall provide a "pipe" communication mechanism allowing immediate message passing through the network (NX-REQ-067).
The system shall provide an **API** for "pipe" communication (NX-REQ-068).
The system shall provide the capability to create "pipes" which "broadcast" their messages to all virtual groups (NX-REQ-069).

The system shall provide error handling information as feedback to "pipe" **API** calls, including notification of invalid parameters, broadcast pipe creation errors with an **ICP** as sender, "open" endpoint errors, message overwrites, connection/transmission errors, creation/open failures, and truncated messages (NX-REQ-070).
The system shall, for non-broadcast "pipes", only allow a single task living on each specified virtual group to "open" the respective end of the pipe (NX-REQ-073).
The system shall, in the case of a broadcast "pipe", allow one task in each virtual group of the system to open the receiving end of the "pipe" (NX-REQ-074).

systemFeatures.name: Fault Detection and Isolation (FDI)
systemFeatures.description: This feature is responsible for the continuous monitoring and rapid identification of hardware and software anomalies within the **nasa x38** system. It encompasses initial power-on diagnostics (**IBIT**), continuous background checks (**Continuous BIT**), and memory scrubbing (**RAM Scrub**) to detect both permanent and transient faults. Upon fault detection, the system aims to isolate the failure and prevent its propagation, making error types and context data available to applications for comprehensive telemetry reporting. This proactive fault management is vital for maintaining the high reliability and safety required in avionics systems.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: System powers on. Response: The system executes Initial Built-In Tests (**IBIT**) on critical hardware components like **FCPs**, **ICPs**, and **MPCCs**.
Stimulus: System operates in normal mode. Response: The system continuously performs background checks (**Continuous BIT**) and memory scrubbing (**RAM Scrub**) to detect latent or transient faults.

Stimulus: A fault (hardware or software) is detected. Response: The system identifies the error type, gathers relevant context data (e.g., **MSR**, **SRR0**, **SRR1**), and makes this information available to the application for telemetry.
functionalRequirements: The system shall perform Initial Built-In Tests (**IBIT**) for all **FCP** and **ICP** components upon system initialization (NX-REQ-075).
The system shall perform Continuous Built-In Tests (**Continuous BIT**) during normal operation to detect latent hardware faults (NX-REQ-076).
The system shall implement a **RAM Scrub** mechanism to detect and correct single-bit errors in memory (NX-REQ-077).

The system shall make the error type and its context data available to the application, via an **API** service, for incorporation in the telemetry stream and include all context data relevant to the exception, namely the contents of the Machine State Register (MSR), and the machine status Save/Restore Registers (SRR0 & SRR1) (NX-REQ-172).
The system shall provide a mechanism for a task optionally to define a user written software-exception-handling routine that runs in the context of the task (NX-REQ-031).

The system shall, for hardware exceptions and reserved exceptions, make the error type and its context data available to the application, then return from the exception handler to the task that was running when the exception occurred (NX-REQ-276).

The system shall, for software exceptions occurring within the FTSS, make the error type and its context data available to the application, then restart the offending task at its beginning (NX-REQ-277).

The system shall, for other software exceptions, regardless of whether or not a user written exception handling routine is invoked, after making available the error type and context data to the application, resume processing at the initialization point of the offending task (NX-REQ-173).
The system shall, for software exceptions occurring during Startup, issue a **VME** reset to the **FCR** in which the exception occurred (NX-REQ-301).

systemFeatures.name: Redundancy Management and Recovery
systemFeatures.description: This feature ensures the continuous availability and resilience of the **nasa x38** system by actively managing redundancy and orchestrating recovery processes. It involves configuring and monitoring virtual groups of processors to maintain fault tolerance, even after component failures. The system's ability to gracefully recover from processor, communication link, and network element failures is paramount for mission success, allowing for continued operation with reduced redundancy. This capability is the cornerstone of the fault-tolerant design, ensuring that the system can sustain critical functions despite hardware or communication anomalies.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A processor experiences a permanent failure. Response: The system detects the failure, enters the Fault Recovery state, and reconfigures the virtual group to isolate the failed processor and maintain operation with remaining healthy units.

Stimulus: A communication link failure is detected between system components. Response: The system initiates a link recovery procedure, rerouting communications or utilizing redundant links to restore connectivity.

Stimulus: A network element failure occurs. Response: The system identifies the failed **NE**, reconfigures communication paths to bypass the element, and updates the virtual group configuration to reflect the change.

Stimulus: A single permanent fault has been isolated and resolved. Response: The system transitions from Fault Recovery back to Normal Operation, now capable of handling another fault with the current redundancy level.
functionalRequirements: The system shall manage the configuration of virtual groups to maintain quad-redundancy for **FCPs** and simplex operation for **ICPs** (NX-REQ-078).
The system shall support recovery from processor failures, maintaining system operation with remaining healthy processors (NX-REQ-079).
The system shall support recovery from communication link failures between system components (NX-REQ-080).
The system shall support recovery from network element failures, ensuring continued communication capabilities (NX-REQ-081).
The system shall, during recovery from processor failure, isolate the failed processor and dynamically reconfigure the virtual group (NX-REQ-082).
The system shall, upon detection of a single permanent fault, reconfigure to maintain fault tolerance such that it can handle an additional fault after recovery (NX-REQ-083).
The system shall manage **Virtual Group** configuration dynamically based on the operational status of member processors and network elements (NX-REQ-084).

systemFeatures.name: Time Services
systemFeatures.description: This feature ensures that all system components operate under a unified and highly accurate time reference. By integrating with **IRIG-B** sources and maintaining internal synchronization, it provides precise mission elapsed time (**MET**) and separation elapsed time (**SEP**) values across all processors. Consistent time stamping and scheduling are critical for the deterministic behavior of the fault-tolerant system and for accurate data logging and event correlation. This precision is essential for coordinating actions across redundant processors and for post-mission analysis.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: The system requires a time synchronization update. Response: The system accesses **IRIG-B** data to update and synchronize internal clocks across all **FCPs** and **ICPs**.

Stimulus: An application task requests current time information. Response: The system provides the synchronized mission elapsed time (**MET**) and separation elapsed time (**SEP**) to the requesting task.
Stimulus: A 50 Hz interrupt is issued. Response: The system ensures all **ICPs** receive the interrupt with minimal skew and alongside synchronized time data.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide synchronized time services to all **FCPs** and **ICPs** (NX-REQ-085).
The system shall provide Mission Elapsed Time (**MET**) and Separation Elapsed Time (**SEP**) values to application tasks (NX-REQ-086).
The system shall interface with **IRIG-B** sources for accurate external time synchronization (NX-REQ-087).
The system shall maintain time synchronization across all operational **FCPs** with minimal drift and jitter (NX-REQ-088).
The system shall send the minor frame number, vehicle mode, mission elapsed time (MET), and separation elapsed time (SEP) to the **ICP** prior to the 50 Hz interrupt (NX-REQ-033).

systemFeatures.name: System Support Services
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides essential infrastructure for the overall management and monitoring of the **nasa x38** system. It handles the critical tasks of telemetry data generation and command processing, enabling external systems and operators to assess system health and issue operational directives. The services ensure that vital diagnostic and operational data are accurately collected and transmitted, while also securely receiving and interpreting commands. This capability is fundamental for remote monitoring, diagnostic capabilities, and ensuring the system responds correctly to external control, critical for an autonomous vehicle.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: An application generates telemetry data. Response: The system collects, formats, and transmits the telemetry data stream via specified communication interfaces (**CTC** requirements).

Stimulus: A command is received from an external source. Response: The system processes the command, validates its integrity, and dispatches it to the appropriate internal module for execution (Command Read requirements).
Stimulus: A rate group over-run occurs. Response: The system incorporates details of the over-run into the telemetry data stream, ensuring diagnostic information is available externally.
functionalRequirements: The system shall meet all defined Central Telemetry and Command (**CTC**) requirements for data transmission (NX-REQ-089).
The system shall meet all defined telemetry requirements for collecting and transmitting system status and diagnostic data (NX-REQ-090).
The system shall meet all defined command read requirements for receiving and interpreting external commands (NX-REQ-091).
The system shall report rate group over-runs to the application via an **API** service for incorporation in the telemetry data stream (NX-REQ-029).
The system shall provide an **API** call to specify which task was running within the rate group which over-ran for telemetry purposes (NX-REQ-216).

systemFeatures.name: Power Down Services
systemFeatures.description: This feature manages the controlled shutdown of the **nasa x38** system, ensuring that all processes are terminated safely, critical data is preserved, and hardware is gracefully powered off. A structured power-down sequence is vital for preventing data corruption, minimizing wear on components, and preparing the system for subsequent re-initialization. This capability supports mission integrity by allowing for predictable and safe cessation of operations, whether planned or in response to an anomaly.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A command for system power down is received. Response: The system initiates a controlled shutdown sequence, stopping application tasks and securing system resources.
Stimulus: All critical data has been secured and system processes halted. Response: The system signals the hardware to perform a safe power-off sequence.
functionalRequirements: The system shall execute a controlled shutdown sequence upon receiving a power-down command (NX-REQ-092).
The system shall ensure all critical data is preserved before hardware power-off (NX-REQ-093).
The system shall gracefully cease all active processes during shutdown (NX-REQ-094).

performanceRequirements: The **nasa x38** system shall complete System Initialization, from hardware reset to starting the 50 Hz timer, in no longer than 1.5 minutes (NX-REQ-015). This is critical for rapid system readiness after power-up or reset events.

The system shall complete a vehicle mode change within 1.02 seconds from notification of an impending change to the start of tasking in the new mode (NX-REQ-020). This ensures responsiveness to dynamic mission requirements.

The system shall execute scheduler and Time Services FTSS overhead tasks in each rate group within 1 millisecond (NX-REQ-034). This guarantees sufficient time for application tasks within the 20 msec minor frame.

The system shall issue the 50 Hz interrupt to all **ICPs** with a skew no greater than 330 microseconds (NX-REQ-191). This is vital for maintaining tight synchronization across distributed processors.

The system shall provide rate groups with a drift rate no greater than 50 microseconds per second and a jitter no greater than 330 microseconds (NX-REQ-035). This ensures deterministic and precise task execution timing.
The system shall detect a babbling **NE** or **ICP** within 20 milliseconds of receiving the first erroneous packet (NX-REQ-235). Rapid detection is crucial for fault isolation.
The system shall mask out a babbling **NE** or **ICP** within 40 milliseconds after its detection (NX-REQ-255). Prompt masking prevents further corruption of system-wide data.
safetyRequirements: The **nasa x38** system shall implement robust fault detection, isolation, and recovery mechanisms to prevent single-point failures from escalating into catastrophic events (NX-REQ-095). This is paramount for the safety of the X-38 Crew Return Vehicle, ensuring system integrity even in adverse conditions.

The system shall provide deterministic behavior for all synchronous tasks and communication operations (NX-REQ-043). Predictability is essential in safety-critical avionics to guarantee expected responses and avoid unexpected states.

The system shall ensure that memory protection mechanisms prevent unauthorized access or modification of critical memory regions (NX-REQ-096). This safeguards against software errors corrupting vital system data or code, maintaining operational safety.

The system shall provide a controlled power-down sequence to prevent data loss or hardware damage during system shutdown (NX-REQ-097). This protects system assets and ensures readiness for subsequent operations.
securityRequirements: The **nasa x38** system shall protect internal communications and data integrity to prevent unauthorized access or tampering (NX-REQ-098). In an avionics context, this safeguards against malicious interference or unintended data corruption.

The system shall implement secure API access control mechanisms to ensure only authorized application components can invoke FTSS services (NX-REQ-099). This protects the core system services from unauthorized or erroneous calls by application software.

The system shall log all critical security-related events and anomalies, such as attempted unauthorized access or integrity breaches, for post-event analysis (NX-REQ-100). This supports forensic investigation and continuous improvement of security posture.

The system shall ensure that no sensitive operational data is exposed through external interfaces without proper encryption or secure protocols (NX-REQ-101). This protects mission-critical information from interception or exploitation.
softwareQualityAttributes: Reliability: The **nasa x38** system shall demonstrate a high level of reliability, with an estimated Mean Time Between Failures (**MTBF**) appropriate for crewed spaceflight systems (NX-REQ-102). This ensures continuous and uninterrupted operation under expected conditions, vital for mission success.

Availability: The system shall maintain high availability through its redundancy management and fault recovery features, aiming for N-1 or N-2 redundancy for critical functions (NX-REQ-103). This minimizes downtime and ensures that critical services remain operational even after component failures.

Maintainability: The system's codebase shall be well-documented and modular, facilitating easy diagnosis, modification, and updates by maintenance personnel (NX-REQ-104). This reduces the effort and time required for bug fixes and future enhancements.

Testability: The system shall be designed with clear interfaces and logging capabilities to support comprehensive testing of all functional and non-functional requirements (NX-REQ-105). This ensures that all requirements can be verified and validated effectively during development and integration.

Robustness: The system shall gracefully handle unexpected inputs, environmental disturbances, and component failures without crashing or entering an unsafe state (NX-REQ-106). This includes mechanisms like watchdog timers, exception handling, and fault isolation to ensure continued operation under adverse conditions.

Adaptability: The system shall be designed to allow for future modifications to task schedules, rate groups, and vehicle modes with minimal impact on the core architecture (NX-REQ-107). This enables the system to evolve with changing mission requirements or new application functionalities.

Usability: The **API** provided by the FTSS shall be intuitive and well-documented for application developers, minimizing the learning curve and potential for integration errors (NX-REQ-108). This enhances developer productivity and reduces the risk of incorrect usage.

Interoperability: The system shall seamlessly interface with the **VxWorks** operating system and specified hardware components like **Network Elements** and **MPCCs** (NX-REQ-109). This ensures correct data exchange and control flow with external software and hardware elements.
businessRules: The **nasa x38** system shall comply with all applicable **NASA** and aerospace industry standards for software development, safety-critical systems, and fault-tolerant computing (NX-REQ-110). This ensures regulatory compliance and adherence to best practices in aerospace engineering.

The system shall prioritize mission-critical functions and data paths, ensuring their continued operation and integrity above less critical services during fault conditions (NX-REQ-111). This dictates resource allocation and recovery strategies in adverse scenarios.

All software and hardware interfaces shall adhere to predefined specifications and protocols, as outlined in referenced documents such as MIL-STD-498 and specific hardware manuals (NX-REQ-112). This ensures compatibility and predictable interactions within the complex avionics system.

Any modifications to the FTSS CSCI, including software updates or configuration changes, shall undergo rigorous verification and validation processes before deployment (NX-REQ-113). This mitigates risks associated with changes in a safety-critical environment.

otherRequirements: ICP Services: The **nasa x38** system shall provide a reduced set of FTSS Communication Services to each Instrument Control Processor (**ICP**) to serve as an **API** between I/O software and **Network Elements** (NX-REQ-114).

Packaging Requirements: The software shall be deliverable in a format compatible with the target hardware's boot processes and software loading mechanisms, ensuring ease of deployment and configuration management (NX-REQ-115).

Precedence and Criticality of Requirements: In the event of conflicting requirements, those pertaining to system safety, fault tolerance, and mission-critical functionality shall take precedence over other requirements (NX-REQ-116). All requirements shall be categorized by criticality (High, Medium, Low) to guide development and testing efforts.

glossary.definition: Application Programming Interface; a set of defined methods for communication between software components.
Board Support Package; software that enables an operating system to interact with specific hardware.
Built-In Test; diagnostic routines embedded in hardware or software to check functionality.
Byzantine Resilient Virtual Circuit; a communication abstraction providing fault-tolerant message delivery.
Computer Software Configuration Item; a distinct unit of software that is managed as a single entity.
Central Telemetry and Command; the system responsible for collecting telemetry data and processing commands.
Digital I/O; Digital Input/Output interfaces for hardware.
Flight Critical Computer; the central quad-redundant computer system in the avionics architecture.
Flight Critical Processor; one of four processors comprising the quad-redundant Flight Critical Computer.
Fault Tolerant Parallel Processor; the underlying architecture providing fault tolerance.
Fault Tolerant System Services; the software CSCI providing core system functionality.
Initial Built-In Test; diagnostics performed at power-on.
Instrument Control Processor; simplex processors responsible for I/O control.
Inter-Range Instrumentation Group Time Code, Format B; a standard for time code synchronization.
Mission Elapsed Time; the time passed since the start of a mission.
Multi-Protocol Communications Controller; a hardware component for handling various communication protocols.
Machine State Register; a processor register containing the current operating state.
Network Element; a component providing network communication services between processors.
Random Access Memory; volatile computer memory.
Single Event Upset; a change of state caused by one ionizing particle.
Separation Elapsed Time; time passed since a significant event or separation.
Software Requirements Specification / Interface Requirements Specification; a document defining software and interface requirements.
Save/Restore Registers; processor registers used to save machine state during exceptions.
Virtual Group; a collection of processors or components operating as a fault-tolerant unit.
Versa Module Eurocard bus; a computer bus standard, often used in real-time embedded systems.
A real-time operating system (RTOS) developed by Wind River Systems.
A Crew Return Vehicle developed by NASA.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: FORMAL SPECIFICATION: Flowcharts describe processes or sequences of operations. They consist of nodes representing steps, decisions, or start/end points, and arrows indicating flow. Node shapes convey meaning (e.g., rounded rectangles for start/end, rectangles for process steps, diamonds for decisions). Labels with spaces or symbols must be enclosed in double-quotes. Node IDs must be alphanumeric and unique, avoiding reserved words like 'end', 'subgraph', or 'class'. Direction must be explicitly stated as 'TD' (top-down) or 'LR' (left-right).
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    Init["System Initialization"]
    Normal["Normal Operation"]
    FaultRec["Fault Recovery"]

    Start(["Power On / Reset"]) --> Init
    Init --> |"Configured & Timer Enabled"| Normal
    Normal --> |"Reset Exception"| Init
    Normal --> |"Fault Detected"| FaultRec
    FaultRec --> |"Recovery Complete"| Normal
flowchartDiagram.caption: nasa x38 System Operational States Flowchart

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: FORMAL SPECIFICATION: Sequence Diagrams illustrate the temporal order of interactions between participants. Participants are declared with 'participant <ID> as "<Alias>"' (ID must be unquoted). Messages are represented by arrows: '->>' for synchronous, '-->>' for asynchronous replies. Message labels describe the communication and must be quoted if they contain spaces. Lifelines represent the existence of a participant over time. Activation bars indicate when a participant is active. Strict compliance dictates avoiding 'activate'/'deactivate' keywords unless simulating complex recursive calls, preferring '->>+' and '-->>-' for automatic activation management. Blocks like 'alt', 'opt', 'loop' group conditional or repetitive interactions, but must not contain explicit activation/deactivation.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant BR as "Boot ROM"
    participant FCP as "Flight Critical Processor"
    participant BSP as "VxWorks BSP"
    participant FTSS as "FTSS System Init"
    participant App as "Application"
    participant ICP as "Instrument Control Processor"
    participant NE as "Network Element"

    BR->>FCP: "IBIT Complete, Call BSP"
    FCP->>BSP: "Initialize Hardware"
    BSP-->>FCP: "BSP Init Complete"
    FCP->>FTSS: "Call System Initialization"
    FTSS->>FCP: "Initiate Watchdog Timer"
    FTSS->>FCP: "Synchronize Virtual Group"
    FTSS->>App: "Call Application Init Function"
    App-->>FTSS: "Tasks and Sockets Created"
    FTSS->>NE: "FCP Ready Sync Message"
    NE->>ICP: "FCP Ready Sync Message"
    ICP->>NE: "ICP Ready Signal"
    NE->>FTSS: "ICP Ready Signal"
    FTSS->>FCP: "Start 50Hz Timer Interrupt"
    FCP-->>FTSS: "Timer Interrupt Enabled"
    FTSS->>App: "Initiate Normal Activities"
sequenceDiagram.caption: nasa x38 System Initialization Sequence

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: FORMAL SPECIFICATION: Entity Relationship Diagrams (ERDs) model the logical structure of data, showing entities (tables), their attributes, and relationships. Entities are declared with curly braces containing attributes. Attributes are defined as 'type name [PK|FK|UK] ["comment"]'. Only 'PK' (Primary Key), 'FK' (Foreign Key), and 'UK' (Unique Key) are allowed, and multiple keys must be comma-separated. Attribute names must be alphanumeric without spaces or quotes. Entity names containing spaces must be double-quoted. Relationships are defined as 'ENTITY1 cardinality1--cardinality2 ENTITY2 : "label"'. Cardinalities (e.g., '||--o{', '}|--||') specify the number of instances involved. Relationship labels must be double-quoted. Foreign Key relationships imply a many-to-one cardinality from the FK-holding entity to the PK-holding entity.
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: nasa x38 Core System Entities and Relationships

tbdList: NX-REQ-TBD-001: The specific performance metrics for the MTBF of the **nasa x38** system need to be formally defined (NX-REQ-102).
NX-REQ-TBD-002: Detailed specifications for the types of errors and context data provided for hardware and reserved exceptions need to be fully enumerated (NX-REQ-276).
NX-REQ-TBD-003: The precise mechanisms for securing **API** access and protecting internal communications require further architectural definition (NX-REQ-098, NX-REQ-099).
NX-REQ-TBD-004: The full scope and content of the logging for security-related events require definition, including storage, access, and retention policies (NX-REQ-100).

NX-REQ-TBD-005: The detailed **CTC** (Central Telemetry and Command) requirements, including message formats, data rates, and protocols, need to be documented in a separate interface specification (NX-REQ-089, NX-REQ-090, NX-REQ-091).
NX-REQ-TBD-006: The specific formats and content for the power-down command and the hardware power-off signal require detailed interface documentation (NX-REQ-092).

introduction.purpose: The **central trading system** (CTS) is designed to facilitate the complete lifecycle of stock trading operations. Its primary goal is to analyze incoming trading instructions, categorize them, and execute matches based on predefined rules. Furthermore, the system is responsible for generating and dispatching messages to various other integrated modules within the broader stock trading ecosystem. This system is crucial for enabling efficient and rule-based stock transactions.
introduction.documentConventions: This Software Requirements Specification (SRS) adheres strictly to the guidelines and recommendations set forth by IEEE 830-1998, 'Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications'. All requirements are expressed in a clear, unambiguous, and testable manner. Standard terminology and naming conventions are applied consistently throughout the document to ensure uniformity and ease of understanding for all stakeholders.
introduction.intendedAudience: This document is intended for a diverse audience, including project managers, system architects, software developers, quality assurance engineers, and technical writers involved in the development and maintenance of the **central trading system**. It also serves as a critical reference for business analysts and stakeholders who need a comprehensive understanding of the system's functional and non-functional requirements. The detailed specifications provided herein will guide implementation, testing, and future enhancements.
introduction.productScope: The **central trading system** operates as a critical subsystem within a larger, comprehensive stock trading system, integrating with several other key modules. Its scope encompasses the reception of trading instructions, their validation and processing, the execution of trades through a matching engine, and the dissemination of transaction results to other connected subsystems. The system is responsible for managing diverse instruction types, ensuring data integrity, and facilitating seamless communication while operating within defined performance and security parameters. It does not directly manage user accounts or external client interfaces.
references: IEEE 830-1998 IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.
ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Systems and software engineering — Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering.

overallDescription.productPerspective: The **central trading system** is conceived as a fundamental subsystem integral to a larger stock trading system (STS) ecosystem. It functions as an intermediary component, receiving instructions from the **trade client serve** and subsequently generating new messages for the **network message promulgating** module after processing. This intricate relationship necessitates robust communication protocols and data exchange mechanisms between the **central trading system** and other subsystems, including **stock account operation**, **financing account operation**, and **trading system manage**. The **central trading system** is not a standalone product but a core engine within this interconnected architecture, ensuring that all trading instructions are processed efficiently and accurately within the broader market context.
productFunctions: The **central trading system** will analyze and categorize incoming trading instructions.
The system will execute buy and sell orders through a sophisticated matching engine.
The system will manage the lifecycle of trading instructions, including cancellation and expiration.
The system will record and maintain comprehensive trade information for auditing and reporting.
The system will provide interfaces for querying trade data and releasing it to external modules.
The system will implement validation rules, such as rising and falling limits, for all trade instructions.
The system will provide real-time feedback on trade execution status to client services.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: These users interact with the **central trading system** indirectly through a graphical user interface provided by the **Transaction User Interface** subsystem. They require simplified operations, primarily involving mouse clicks and keyboard inputs to submit trading instructions. Their technical expertise is minimal, focusing on the ease of use and responsiveness of the client-side application rather than the underlying system complexities. They expect clear feedback on their trading activities and the status of their orders.

These users are highly technical personnel responsible for the continuous operation, monitoring, and troubleshooting of the **central trading system**. They must possess strong expertise in **Java programming** and **socket programming** to diagnose system crashes, implement fixes, and adapt the system to evolving requirements. Maintainers are tasked with ensuring system stability under heavy load, optimizing performance, and developing strategies to prevent system overloads, requiring deep understanding of the system's internal architecture and operational parameters.

These are other modules within the larger stock trading system that interact with the **central trading system** via defined interfaces. This class includes **Transaction User Interface**, **Security Account Management**, **Trading Management System**, and **Trading Information Release**. They act as programmatic users, exchanging data and invoking services. Their primary characteristic is their reliance on the **central trading system** for core trading logic and data, requiring robust, well-documented APIs for seamless integration and reliable data exchange. They operate under strict communication protocols.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The **central trading system** is designed to operate within a distributed, multi-component architecture, serving as a critical backend service. The system's core logic will be implemented using **Java**, leveraging robust capabilities for concurrent processing and network communication. It is anticipated to run on enterprise-grade server hardware, ensuring high availability and processing capacity. The underlying operating system will be a stable **Linux** distribution, chosen for its reliability and performance in server environments.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The system must adhere to a modular architecture to facilitate integration with existing and future subsystems within the overall stock trading system. All inter-subsystem communication, particularly with the **trade client serve** and **network message promulgating** modules, must utilize standardized **socket programming** interfaces to ensure efficient and reliable data exchange.

Given the frequently operated instructions such as fetch, deal with, and repeal, the **central trading system** is expected to handle heavy data transfer and processing loads. The implementation must incorporate strategies to prevent system crashes and performance degradation when processing overhead approaches or exceeds its nominal capacity, potentially requiring advanced load balancing and error handling mechanisms.

Specific instruction parameters, as defined for buy, sell, and query instructions (user ID, stock ID, quantity, respected price, timestamp, query content, restrict parameters), must be strictly adhered to in all incoming and outgoing messages. Any deviation in message structure or content will be considered an invalid instruction and must be handled gracefully by the system.
userDocumentation: User manuals will be provided for Terminal Users, detailing how to interact with the **Transaction User Interface** for submitting and monitoring trading instructions. These manuals will focus on step-by-step procedures, common scenarios, and troubleshooting tips related to client-side operations.

Comprehensive online help and tutorials will be integrated into the client-side application, offering immediate assistance for users navigating the trading functionalities and understanding various instruction types and their statuses.

Technical documentation, including API specifications, system architecture diagrams, and maintenance guides, will be developed for System Maintainers. This documentation will cover **Java programming** standards, **socket communication protocols**, database schemas, and detailed recovery procedures for system crashes.
assumptionsAndDependencies: It is assumed that the external **Transaction User Interface** subsystem will reliably capture user inputs and correctly format them into valid trading instructions before transmitting them to the **central trading system**. The integrity of incoming data is crucial for the system's operational accuracy.

The **central trading system** is highly dependent on the availability and responsiveness of the **Security Account Management** and **Trading Management System** subsystems for critical operations such as fund freezing, account updates, and overall system oversight. Failures or delays in these dependent systems will directly impact the **central trading system's** ability to process transactions.

The continuous operation of network infrastructure is assumed for reliable communication between all subsystems. Any network latency or instability could significantly degrade the performance and reliability of trade processing within the **central trading system**.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: The **central trading system** itself does not directly expose a graphical user interface to end-users. Instead, it interfaces with the **Transaction User Interface** subsystem, which handles all user-facing interactions. This client interface provides simplified operations for terminal users, primarily relying on mouse clicks and keyboard inputs to submit trading instructions. Standard GUI elements, consistent layout constraints, and predefined buttons and functions will be implemented by the **Transaction User Interface** to ensure a cohesive user experience.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The **central trading system** will primarily interact with standard networking hardware components, including network interface cards (NICs), routers, and switches, to facilitate communication with other subsystems. All data and control interactions will occur over standard network protocols. No direct, specialized hardware interfaces (e.g., custom peripherals, embedded sensors) are required beyond those necessary for standard server operation and network connectivity. The system relies on the operating system's abstraction layers for hardware management.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The **central trading system** communicates with several critical software components within the broader stock trading ecosystem. It receives instructions from the **Trade Client Serve** and sends messages to the **Network Message Promulgating** module. It also interacts with a dedicated **Database** for storing and retrieving instruction, trade, and log information. Furthermore, it exchanges data with **Security Account Management** for fund operations and **Trading Information Release** for data dissemination. All these interactions will utilize clearly defined APIs, passing structured messages containing parameters such as user ID, stock ID, quantity, price, and timestamps. Detailed interface specifications, including data formats and communication protocols, will be documented separately to ensure seamless integration and interoperability.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: The **central trading system** requires robust communication functions to interact with various internal and external modules. Communication between the **central trading system** and other subsystems will predominantly occur over TCP/IP **sockets**, ensuring reliable, connection-oriented data transfer. Specific message formatting, including header and payload structures, will be defined for buy, sell, query, and cancellation instructions, as well as for trade results and system status updates. While explicit security or encryption issues were noted as 'open issues' in use cases, all communications must be secured to protect sensitive trading data. Data transfer rates must support frequent updates, ensuring near real-time processing of trading instructions.

systemFeatures.name: Instruction Pre-treatment and Validation
systemFeatures.description: This feature is crucial for ensuring the integrity and validity of all incoming trading instructions before they enter the core matching engine. It provides user value by preventing erroneous or non-compliant instructions from affecting the trading process, thereby reducing potential financial risks. Technically, it acts as a primary filter, performing critical checks such as validating instruction parameters, enforcing rising and falling price limits, freezing user funds, and logging all instruction activities. This module significantly contributes to system stability and regulatory compliance. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: **Transaction User Interface** sends a buy/sell instruction. Response: **central trading system** receives and performs initial validation.
Stimulus: An instruction's price exceeds the rising limit. Response: **central trading system** rejects the instruction.
Stimulus: A valid instruction is received. Response: **central trading system** initiates freezing of the user's funds in the **Security Account Management** system and logs the event.
functionalRequirements: The system shall receive buy, sell, and query instructions from the **Trade Client Serve**.
The system shall validate all incoming instructions for completeness, format, and adherence to business rules.
The system shall check if the instruction's price adheres to the defined rising and falling limits.
The system shall reject instructions that violate price limits or other validation rules.
The system shall initiate the freezing of funds for valid buy/sell instructions with the **Security Account Management** subsystem.
The system shall log all incoming instructions and their validation outcomes.
The system shall pass validated and pre-processed instructions to the Instruction Manager.

systemFeatures.name: Instruction Matching Engine
systemFeatures.description: The instruction matching engine is the core component for executing trades, directly translating user buy and sell instructions into completed transactions. Its user value lies in providing a fair and efficient mechanism for trade execution, ensuring that orders are matched according to established market principles. From a technical perspective, this feature implements sophisticated algorithms to match instructions based on 'price first' and 'time first' principles, continuously processing a dynamic list of orders. It modifies instruction statuses and generates trade records, acting as the heart of the **central trading system**. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: **central trading system** receives a valid buy instruction. Response: The system searches for matching sell instructions based on price and time priority.
Stimulus: A matching buy and sell instruction are identified. Response: **central trading system** executes the trade, modifies the information of matched instructions, and records the transaction.
Stimulus: A trade transaction is successfully conducted. Response: **central trading system** generates trade information for saving and releasing.
functionalRequirements: The system shall maintain a real-time list of active buy and sell instructions.
The system shall apply a 'price first' principle for matching buy and sell instructions.
The system shall apply a 'time first' principle for matching instructions with identical prices.
The system shall make a match between instructions if the lowest buy price is higher than the highest sell price.
The system shall update the status and remaining quantity of partially fulfilled instructions.
The system shall generate a unique trade record for every successful transaction.
The system shall notify the **Security Account Management** subsystem of completed trades for fund settlement.

systemFeatures.name: Instruction Cancellation and Expiration
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides users with the flexibility to manage their submitted orders by allowing the cancellation of active instructions. This enhances user control and reduces potential losses from unwanted trades. Technically, the system must efficiently locate and remove specific instructions from the active order list, handling cases where the instruction has already been partially or fully executed. It also includes mechanisms for automatically removing 'outdated' instructions, ensuring the order book remains current and relevant. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: **Transaction User Interface** sends a cancel instruction. Response: **central trading system** receives and attempts to cancel the corresponding instruction.
Stimulus: A cancel instruction is received for an active, unmatched order. Response: **central trading system** removes the instruction from the trading list.
Stimulus: An instruction has not been fully traded within one day. Response: **central trading system** automatically removes the instruction as outdated.
functionalRequirements: The system shall receive cancel instructions from the **Trade Client Serve**.
The system shall locate the instruction to be cancelled based on provided identifiers.
The system shall cancel an instruction if it has not yet been fully implemented.
The system shall prevent the cancellation of instructions that have already been fully executed.
The system shall automatically remove any instruction that has not completed its trading within a single day.
The system shall log all cancellation attempts and their outcomes.

systemFeatures.name: Trade Information Management
systemFeatures.description: This feature ensures that all successful trade transactions are accurately and persistently stored within the system, providing a reliable historical record. This delivers significant user value by enabling auditing, reporting, and regulatory compliance. Technically, it involves the **central trading system** securely transmitting confirmed trade data to the **Security Account Management** subsystem for saving, and possibly integrating with a persistent data store. This guarantees data integrity and availability for future analysis and client inquiries. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: A trade transaction is successfully conducted by the matching engine. Response: **central trading system** gives out the successful trade information.
Stimulus: **Security Account Management** receives successful trade information. Response: **Security Account Management** saves the trade information persistently.
Stimulus: All operations are suspended. Response: **central trading system** defers sending trade information until operations resume (TBD handling).
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide an interface for the **Security Account Management** subsystem to receive successful trade information.
The system shall ensure that all successful trade information includes relevant details such as instruction IDs, trade price, trade quantity, and trade timestamp.
The system shall ensure data consistency when transmitting trade information to dependent subsystems.
The system shall log all instances of successful trade information transmission.

systemFeatures.name: Trade Information Query and Release
systemFeatures.description: This feature allows authorized subsystems to query and retrieve trade information for statistical analysis and public dissemination. This provides user value by enabling transparency and supporting data-driven decisions for market analysis and reporting. Technically, the **central trading system** must process query requests from the **Trading Information Release** system, efficiently retrieve relevant data from its storage, structure it appropriately, and transmit it back. It ensures that only authorized information is released and maintains the integrity of the data during retrieval and transmission. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: **Trading Information Release** system sends a query for trading information. Response: **central trading system** receives the query and implements it.
Stimulus: A valid query is executed. Response: **central trading system** structuralizes the queried data.
Stimulus: Queried data is structured. Response: **central trading system** sends the data to the **Trading Information Release** system.
functionalRequirements: The system shall receive query instructions from the **Trading Information Release** subsystem.
The system shall support user query instructions with parameters including user ID, query content, and restrict parameters.
The system shall support stock query instructions with parameters including stock ID, query content, and restrict parameters.
The system shall implement queries by referring to the **Database** or internal instruction lists.
The system shall structuralize the queried data into a predefined format for transmission.
The system shall transmit the structured query results to the **Trading Information Release** subsystem.
The system shall log all query requests and their responses.

systemFeatures.name: System Administration and Monitoring
systemFeatures.description: This feature provides necessary interfaces and capabilities for system managers to monitor and manage the **central trading system**, ensuring its optimal performance and security. Its user value is in enabling system administrators to maintain the system effectively, troubleshoot issues, and ensure operational continuity. Technically, this involves granting authorized access to system information, operational logs, and potentially configuration parameters. It supports maintaining system health, auditing activities, and responding to operational incidents. Priority: Medium.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: **Trading System Management** attempts to access information in the **central trading system**. Response: The system validates the authorization of the request.
Stimulus: An authorized manager requests logs. Response: The system provides access to relevant log files, including instruction and trade logs.
Stimulus: A system manager identifies a potential security issue (TBD). Response: The system logs the alert and initiates appropriate response protocols (TBD).
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide authorized access to system information for the **Trading System Management** subsystem.
The system shall provide access to various log files, including instruction, trade, and exception logs, for monitoring purposes.
The system shall allow **Trading System Management** to query user trading instructions based on type and restrict parameters.
The system shall implement robust authentication and authorization mechanisms for all administrative access.
The system shall log all administrative actions and access attempts for auditing.
The system shall allow for graceful shutdown and restart procedures under administrative control.

performanceRequirements: The system shall process a minimum of 1,000 trading instructions per second during peak trading hours to ensure timely order execution. (Rationale: The system is 'heavily transferred' and processes 'frequent' instructions, requiring high throughput to prevent bottlenecks and ensure market responsiveness.)

The latency for matching a new instruction against existing orders shall not exceed 100 milliseconds for 99% of transactions. (Rationale: Low latency is critical for a trading system to ensure fair and immediate execution, aligning with 'price first' and 'time first' principles.)

The system shall maintain a response time for query requests from the **Trading Information Release** system of under 500 milliseconds for 95% of queries. (Rationale: Efficient data retrieval is necessary for timely market analysis and information dissemination, as queries are 'frequent'.)
safetyRequirements: The system shall implement transactional integrity for all trade operations, ensuring that either all steps of a trade (matching, fund update, record creation) are successfully committed, or none are. (Rationale: Prevents partial trades, data corruption, and financial inconsistencies, crucial for a trading system's reliability.)

The system shall incorporate mechanisms for automated data backup and recovery to a separate, secure location at least once every 24 hours. (Rationale: Protects against data loss due to system failures or disasters, ensuring business continuity.)

The system shall gracefully handle erroneous or malformed incoming instructions without crashing or corrupting existing data. (Rationale: Protects system stability and data integrity from external malicious or accidental inputs, addressing potential 'trade exceptions'.)
securityRequirements: The system shall enforce strict authentication and authorization for all access attempts, particularly from the **Trading System Management** and other integrated subsystems, using industry-standard protocols. (Rationale: Addresses 'security issue' mentioned in use cases and protects sensitive financial data from unauthorized access.)

All sensitive data, including user IDs, account balances, and trade parameters, exchanged between subsystems shall be encrypted using AES-256 or higher. (Rationale: Protects data confidentiality during transit across potentially insecure networks, mitigating eavesdropping risks.)

The system shall maintain comprehensive audit logs of all trading instructions, trade executions, data modifications, and administrative actions, securely storing them for a minimum of seven years. (Rationale: Provides non-repudiation and traceability for regulatory compliance and forensic analysis, addressing 'is exception log needed?'.)
softwareQualityAttributes: Reliability: The system shall maintain an uptime of 99.9% during scheduled trading hours. (Rationale: A trading system must be continuously available to process transactions and prevent financial losses due to downtime, especially given its role in a 'whole stock trading system'.)

Availability: The system shall be operational and accessible for processing trading instructions 24 hours a day, 5 days a week (Monday-Friday), excluding scheduled maintenance windows. (Rationale: Ensures continuous market access during trading days, aligning with the frequent nature of trading operations.)

Maintainability: The system's codebase shall be well-documented and modular, allowing for modifications and enhancements with minimal impact on existing functionalities. (Rationale: 'Maintainers' need to 'modify the program to make it fit' new requirements and fix issues efficiently.)

Scalability: The system shall be capable of horizontally scaling to support a 50% increase in instruction processing volume within 6 months, without requiring a complete architectural overhaul. (Rationale: Anticipates future growth and 'heavy transfer' situations, ensuring the system can adapt to increased demand.)

Testability: The system's components shall be designed with clear interfaces and separation of concerns to enable independent unit, integration, and system testing. (Rationale: Facilitates thorough verification and validation, especially for complex 'matching' algorithms and inter-subsystem communications.)
businessRules: All trading instructions shall be processed adhering to the 'price first' principle, prioritizing orders with the best price.
In cases of identical prices, trading instructions shall be matched according to the 'time first' principle, prioritizing earlier orders.
Any buy instruction with a price higher than the predefined rising limit or a sell instruction with a price lower than the falling limit shall be automatically rejected.
If a trading instruction has not been fully executed or cancelled within one trading day, it shall be automatically removed from the active instruction list as 'outdated'.
Partial execution of instructions is permitted, with the remaining quantity maintained and eligible for subsequent matching.

otherRequirements: All time-related parameters (e.g., timestamps, instruction expiration) shall be synchronized with a central time server to ensure consistency across all subsystems and accurate application of time-based trading rules.

glossary.definition: The core subsystem responsible for analyzing, validating, matching, and managing stock trading instructions within the broader stock trading system.
A formal command from a user (via the Transaction User Interface) to buy, sell, or query stock information, including details like quantity, price, and timestamp.
The outcome of processing a user instruction, which is returned to other subsystems to indicate success, failure, or partial execution of a trade.
Specific commands or data flows directed to the Database for operations such as querying, writing, or modifying trade and instruction records.
The most recent outcome of a trade transaction, which is typically sent to the Information Releasing Module for dissemination.
The initial phase of processing an incoming instruction, involving validation, freezing of funds, and logging, before it proceeds to the main instruction manager.
The module responsible for dealing with instructions in detail, including processing queries, cancellations, and routing trade instructions to the matching engine.
The process of recording details about an instruction's lifecycle, status, and any associated events or exceptions for auditing and debugging purposes.
A temporary hold placed on a user's account balance, corresponding to the value of a pending buy or sell instruction, to ensure sufficient funds or shares are available for trade execution.
A type of instruction that involves operations on the internal instruction list, such as adding, deleting, searching, sorting, or making matches with other instructions.
The overarching system of which the central trading system is a subsystem, encompassing all aspects of stock trading operations.
The client-facing subsystem that allows terminal users to interact with the trading system by submitting instructions.
The subsystem responsible for managing user accounts, balances, and executing operations like freezing funds and saving trade information.
The subsystem responsible for querying and disseminating trading information, potentially to a public website or analytical tools.
The subsystem or role responsible for overall system oversight, monitoring, and administrative tasks within the stock trading system.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart illustrates the high-level process flow within the **central trading system**, from receiving an instruction to its final processing and result delivery. Nodes represent processes or decisions, and arrows indicate the flow of control. Decision nodes are diamond-shaped, start/end nodes are rounded rectangles, and process nodes are rectangular. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels with spaces are double-quoted.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
  StartProcess("Start: Receive Instruction") --> ValidateInstruction{"Validate Instruction Parameters?"}
  ValidateInstruction -->|No| RejectInstruction["Reject Instruction & Log Error"]
  ValidateInstruction -->|Yes| CheckLimits{"Check Rising/Falling Limits?"}
  CheckLimits -->|No| RejectInstruction
  CheckLimits -->|Yes| FreezeFunds["Freeze User Funds (via SAM)"]
  FreezeFunds --> LogInstruction["Log Validated Instruction"]
  LogInstruction --> IdentifyType{"Identify Instruction Type?"}
  IdentifyType -->|Query| ProcessQuery["Process Query Instruction"]
  ProcessQuery --> ReadData["Read Data (Database/List)"]
  ReadData --> StructureResult["Structure Query Result"]
  StructureResult --> SendQueryResult["Send Query Result (to TIR)"]
  IdentifyType -->|Cancel| ProcessCancel["Process Cancel Instruction"]
  ProcessCancel --> LocateInstruction["Locate Instruction to Cancel"]
  LocateInstruction --> InstructionStatus{"Instruction Fully Executed?"}
  InstructionStatus -->|Yes| RejectCancel["Reject Cancel & Log"]
  InstructionStatus -->|No| DeleteInstruction["Delete from Instruction List"]
  DeleteInstruction --> LogCancel["Log Cancellation"]
  IdentifyType -->|Trade (Buy/Sell)| ProcessTrade["Process Trade Instruction"]
  ProcessTrade --> SearchMatch["Search for Matching Instructions (Price/Time First)"]
  SearchMatch --> MatchFound{"Match Found?"}
  MatchFound -->|No| AddToList["Add Instruction to Active List"]
  MatchFound -->|Yes| ExecuteTrade["Execute Trade & Update Quantities"]
  ExecuteTrade --> LogTrade["Log Trade & Generate Trade Record"]
  LogTrade --> NotifySAM["Notify SAM for Fund Settlement"]
  NotifySAM --> UpdateTradeStatus["Update Trade Status"]
  UpdateTradeStatus --> SendTradeResult["Send Trade Result (to Client/TIR)"]
  RejectInstruction --> EndProcess("End")
  SendQueryResult --> EndProcess
  LogCancel --> EndProcess
  SendTradeResult --> EndProcess
flowchartDiagram.caption: central trading system Core Process Flow

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram illustrates the typical interaction flow for a 'Buy Stock' transaction within the **central trading system**. Participants are represented by vertical lifelines, and messages passed between them are depicted by horizontal arrows, indicating the temporal order of events. Activations show periods during which a participant is performing an action. Notes provide additional context to specific interactions.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant TUI
  participant CTS
  participant SAM
  participant DB
  
  TUI->>+CTS: "Submit Buy Instruction (StockID, Qty, Price)"
  CTS->>CTS: "Validate Instruction (Price Limits)"
  CTS->>+SAM: "Request Fund Freeze (UserID, Amount)"
  SAM-->>-CTS: "Fund Freeze Confirmation"
  CTS->>DB: "Save Buy Instruction"
  CTS->>CTS: "Search for Matching Sell Orders"
  activate CTS
  CTS->>DB: "Query Active Sell Instructions"
  DB-->>CTS: "Return Matching Sell Orders"
  CTS->>CTS: "Apply Price & Time Priority Matching Logic"
  alt Match Found
    CTS->>DB: "Update Matched Instruction Status"
    CTS->>DB: "Record New Trade Transaction"
    CTS->>+SAM: "Notify Trade Completion (UserID, StockID, Qty, Price)"
    SAM-->>-CTS: "Fund Settlement Confirmation"
    CTS-->>-TUI: "Trade Confirmation (Status: Completed)"
  else No Match
    CTS->>DB: "Add Buy Instruction to Active Order List"
    CTS-->>-TUI: "Order Confirmation (Status: Pending)"
  end
  deactivate CTS
sequenceDiagram.caption: Buy Stock Transaction Sequence

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) visually represents the key entities within the **central trading system** and their relationships. Entities are defined with attributes, including primary (PK) and foreign (FK) keys. Relationships between entities are shown with cardinality (e.g., one-to-many, one-to-one), and descriptive labels are provided for clarity. Attribute names are alphanumeric, and entity names with spaces are quoted.
entityRelationshipDiagram.code:
erDiagram
  USER ||--o{ ACCOUNT : "owns"
  USER ||--o{ INSTRUCTION : "places"
  STOCK ||--o{ INSTRUCTION : "refers_to"
  INSTRUCTION ||--o{ LOG_ENTRY : "generates"
  TRADE }|--|| INSTRUCTION : "has_buy_instruction"
  TRADE }|--|| INSTRUCTION : "has_sell_instruction"
  TRADE ||--o{ LOG_ENTRY : "generates"

  USER {
    int id PK
    string email
    string name
    datetime created_at
  }
  ACCOUNT {
    int id PK
    int user_id FK
    decimal balance
    datetime created_at
  }
  STOCK {
    int id PK
    string symbol
    string name
    decimal current_price
  }
  INSTRUCTION {
    int id PK
    int user_id FK
    int stock_id FK
    string type
    int quantity
    decimal price
    datetime timestamp
    string status
  }
  TRADE {
    int id PK
    int buy_instruction_id FK
    int sell_instruction_id FK
    decimal trade_price
    int trade_quantity
    datetime trade_time
  }
  LOG_ENTRY {
    int id PK
    datetime timestamp
    string event_type
    string message
  }
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: central trading system Data Model
